title: Child mortality
subtitle: The share of children who died in their first five years of life.
note: The reconstructions are mapped into modern country borders. The data is of strongly varying quality across countries and over time. For details see our work on child mortality.
caption:
Child mortality

The share of children who died in their first five years of life.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 child mortality rates, defined as the share of children who died in their first five years of life. The legend ranges from 0% to 30%, with color bands marking 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, and 30%. The darkest red shades, indicating rates above 20%, are concentrated in a cluster of countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Somalia, Chad, the Central African Republic, and Nigeria. Surrounding nations in West and East Africa appear in orange and yellow bands, placing them in the 10% to 20% range. In contrast, the vast majority of the globe is colored in shades of blue. North America, South America, Europe, Australia, and most of Asia sit in the lightest blue bands, corresponding to values below 5%. Within this broad low-mortality group, some variation exists, with certain countries in Eastern Europe and Central Asia appearing in slightly darker blue shades than their Western counterparts. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data is available for that territory. The reconstructions are mapped into modern country borders, and the data is of strongly varying quality across countries and over time. The visual pattern shows the highest mortality shares confined to specific regions in Africa, while the lowest shares characterize most of the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Oceania.
